what_manipulated: degree of dishonest behavior permitted while maintaining self-concept
what_measured: dishonest choices relative to profit opportunity
experiment: Units were tested on their willingness to act dishonestly for financial gain while reporting their self-concept. The study examined two mechanisms: inattention to moral standards (whether lowering moral salience increases dishonesty) and categorization malleability (whether reframing dishonest acts as acceptable permits more dishonesty). Six separate experiments manipulated contexts and measured the magnitude of dishonest choices alongside self-perception maintenance. Tests compared conditions with high versus low moral salience and varying opportunity structures.

what_manipulated: tactile properties of objects incidentally held or manipulated during an unrelated task
what_measured: judgments and decisions about social targets and negotiation outcomes
experiment: Units held or manipulated objects with varied tactile properties (heavy versus light clipboards, rough versus smooth puzzles, hard versus soft objects) while performing an ostensibly unrelated task of forming impressions or making decisions about people or situations. Six experiments tested whether implicit exposure to tactile features influenced social judgments. Conditions were compared using rating scales and behavioral choices; mechanisms tested dimension-specific and metaphor-specific associations between tactile and social domains.

what_manipulated: emotional intensity and the source of emotional cues (facial versus bodily)
what_measured: accuracy of emotion classification (positive versus negative) and perception of mismatched cues
experiment: Observers were shown videos or images of individuals during peak emotional states and were asked to discriminate positive from negative emotions. The study manipulated whether observers saw faces alone, bodies alone, or face-body combinations. Accuracy was measured via classification responses. Follow-up experiments used compound stimuli (negative faces with positive bodies and vice versa) to test whether perceived emotion shifted based on body cues. Results examined patterns of mimicry and illusory affect perception.

what_manipulated: the type of prior episodic memory task (encoding novel items versus retrieval of old items)
what_measured: subsequent memory performance on pattern separation and pattern completion tasks
experiment: Units performed an initial memory task that engaged either encoding (exposure to novel objects) or retrieval (recognition of previously seen items). Subsequently, they completed tasks sensitive to pattern separation (detecting subtle changes) and pattern completion (integrating old information into new memories). The study measured task accuracy and examined how recent encoding versus retrieval biased subsequent memory processing. Comparisons tested whether lingering cognitive biases from the prior task persisted.

what_manipulated: cognitive processing style (analytic versus intuitive, induced or measured as individual difference)
what_measured: strength of religious belief or disbelief
experiment: Four experiments tested whether triggering or measuring analytic processing would reduce religious belief. In correlational analyses, individual differences in analytical reasoning were associated with religious disbelief. In causal experiments, subtle primes known to activate analytic thinking (e.g., exposure to logic puzzles, reflective prompts) preceded belief rating tasks. Changes in belief were measured on rating scales. Comparisons tested the consistency of this relationship across different analytical manipulations and populations.

what_manipulated: the presence of communication and costly punishment as response options in a common-pool resource game
what_measured: resource harvesting rates and aggregate payoffs in a spatial and temporal resource dynamics scenario
experiment: A laboratory commons dilemma was implemented in a spatially and temporally dynamic environment where units made harvesting decisions over multiple rounds. The experiment compared conditions with and without communication access and with and without the ability to punish others (at a cost). Aggregate resource levels and individual payoffs were tracked. Statistical comparisons examined whether costly punishment increased gross benefits and tested the interactive effect of communication plus punishment.

what_manipulated: type of narrative reading material (literary fiction versus popular fiction versus nonfiction versus no reading)
what_measured: performance on tests of theory of mind (affective and cognitive)
experiment: Units were assigned to read one of several passage types or a control condition. Subsequently, they completed standardized tests assessing ability to infer others' emotions (affective theory of mind) and beliefs (cognitive theory of mind). Five experiments tested whether literary fiction reading produced superior performance relative to other reading types and control. Comparisons measured score differences on multiple theory of mind instruments.

what_manipulated: whether an agent's beliefs were congruent or incongruent with the unit's own beliefs in a visual detection task
what_measured: reaction time (adults) and looking time (infants) in response to objects
experiment: Units (adults and seven-month-old infants) performed a visual object detection task where an agent was present. The agent either believed an object was at one location (unit believed it was elsewhere) or both held the same belief. Reaction times or looking times were measured. The unit's behavior was compared when the agent was present versus absent, and when agent beliefs were congruent versus incongruent with the unit's beliefs. Tests examined automatic encoding of others' beliefs independent of task relevance.

what_manipulated: physical cleansing (hand washing) following a decision task
what_measured: postdecisional dissonance (perceived attractiveness gap between chosen and unchosen alternatives)
experiment: Units made a choice between two alternatives and then either washed their hands or engaged in a control activity. Subsequently, they re-rated the attractiveness of both options. The study measured the difference in attractiveness between chosen and unchosen alternatives. Comparisons tested whether the dissonance effect (typically showing increased difference in favor of the chosen option) was reduced or eliminated by physical cleansing.

what_manipulated: frequency of imagined consumption of a food item (many versus few repetitions) and identity of the imagined food (specific versus different item)
what_measured: actual consumption quantity and desire to eat the food
experiment: Five experiments asked units to imagine eating a specific food many times, few times, or to imagine eating an alternative food or nothing. Subsequently, units were offered the food and consumption was measured by weight or count. The study tested whether habituation (reduced intake and reduced desire) occurred following imagined consumption alone. Mechanisms were examined by measuring perceived palatability separately from intake motivation.

what_manipulated: practice method (test-restudy versus restudy only) during learning of foreign language translations
what_measured: quality of mediating associations (retrieval probability and target-elicitation effectiveness) and final test performance
experiment: Units studied foreign language word pairs using either practice tests followed by restudying or restudying only. The study measured whether units generated effective mediators (linking information connecting cue to target) and tested whether mediators were retrievable and elicited correct targets on a final test. Comparisons examined differences in mediator effectiveness between practice conditions using recall and final test accuracy measures.

what_manipulated: expressive writing about test-related worries (intervention versus control) administered immediately before a high-stakes exam
what_measured: exam performance (test scores)
experiment: Units assigned to write about upcoming test anxieties (intervention) or a control task completed a brief writing exercise immediately before taking an important exam. Exam scores were measured and compared between conditions. The study included both laboratory experiments and two randomized field experiments in classroom settings. Analyses examined whether the intervention benefited all students or particularly those with high trait test anxiety.

what_manipulated: scarcity (resource abundance versus scarcity constraints induced through experimental context or task)
what_measured: attention allocation patterns and behavioral choices reflecting overborrowing or financial decisions
experiment: Several experiments manipulated scarcity through time pressure, financial constraints, or puzzle-solving contexts with limited resources. Units' attention was measured (eye tracking or performance on secondary tasks) and their financial decisions were observed. Scarcity conditions were compared to abundance conditions. The study tested whether resource limitations narrowed attentional focus to immediate scarcity problems, causing neglect of other important tasks.

what_manipulated: expectation of future information access (accessible versus inaccessible) during question-answering tasks
what_measured: memory for information content versus memory for information location (transactive memory)
experiment: Four experiments tested whether expecting to have future access to information altered memory encoding. Units answered difficult questions under conditions where they expected to retain access to search tools (high accessibility) or lacked such access (low accessibility). Subsequent memory tests assessed recall of the information itself versus recall of where the information could be found. Comparisons examined differences in memory performance and memory-type focus between conditions.

what_manipulated: availability of external stimuli (presence versus absence; option to self-administer electric shock for stimulation)
what_measured: discomfort with or avoidance of solitary thought; willingness to self-administer unpleasant stimuli
experiment: Units were placed in a quiet room alone with their thoughts for a sustained period and asked to report comfort level. In some conditions, units were offered the option to give themselves mild electric shocks. The study measured how long units remained alone without external stimuli and whether they chose to self-administer shocks despite reported discomfort from the shocks. Patterns were examined across demographic groups.

what_manipulated: physical warmth experience (warm versus cold conditions, or physical warmth applied after rejection)
what_measured: feelings of social warmth or loneliness, and self-regulatory behaviors (bathing frequency, warmth-seeking)
experiment: Four studies examined relationships between physical and social warmth. Study 1 measured chronic loneliness and bathing frequency correlations. Study 2 manipulated physical coldness and measured subsequent loneliness ratings. Study 3 induced rejection (triggering affiliation needs) and applied physical warmth or control, measuring whether warmth reduced affiliation-seeking. Study 4 tested whether units recognized the physical-social warmth connection. Analyses used correlations, t-tests comparing warmth conditions, and subjective reports.

what_manipulated: nonconscious priming of behavioral goals (goal to perform well versus goal to cooperate, or control prime) via word-exposure prior to tasks
what_measured: task performance (intellectual task success) and cooperative behavior (resource replenishment)
experiment: Five experiments primed goals outside of conscious awareness using word-association and subliminal exposure tasks, then measured goal-directed behavior. Experiment 1 primed performance goals and measured intellectual task scores. Experiment 2 primed cooperation and measured resources contributed to a common pool. Experiment 3 ruled out perceptual-construal alternatives using a dissociation paradigm. Experiments 4 and 5 tested whether nonconscious goal pursuit manifested features of conscious goal pursuit (persistence, adaptation).

what_manipulated: temporal ordering: standard effect (stimulus then response) versus time-reversed (response before stimulus) within established psychological paradigms
what_measured: effect size of time-reversed phenomena across multiple domains (precognitive approach/avoidance, priming, habituation, recall)
experiment: Nine experiments tested for retroactive influences by presenting response measures before stimulus events in paradigms with established psychological effects. More than 1,000 units participated across four types of time-reversed effects: approach to future erotic stimuli, avoidance of future negative stimuli, retroactive priming, retroactive habituation, and retroactive facilitation of recall. Effect sizes were calculated and examined for statistical significance. Individual differences in stimulus-seeking (extraversion component) were tested as a correlate of effect size.

what_manipulated: postural display (high-power expansive posture versus low-power contractive posture held for 1-2 minutes)
what_measured: testosterone levels, cortisol levels, subjective feelings of power, and risk tolerance
experiment: Units assumed either high-power postures (arms raised, legs spread, open chest) or low-power postures (arms crossed, legs together, closed body) for one to two minutes. Blood samples measured testosterone and cortisol before and after posturing. Units rated subjective power feelings and made behavioral choices measuring risk tolerance (e.g., gambling decisions). Comparisons tested whether postural displays caused endocrine changes and downstream behavioral and psychological shifts.

what_manipulated: type of positive affect stimulus (high approach-motivated [e.g., appetitive] versus low approach-motivated [e.g., nurturing]) or trait approach motivation
what_measured: breadth of attentional focus (global versus local processing)
experiment: Four studies examined attentional breadth following exposure to positive-affect stimuli varying in approach motivation. Study 1 showed reduced global focus after high-approach stimuli versus low-approach stimuli. Study 2 showed reduced global focus after high-approach stimuli versus neutral stimuli. Study 3 correlated trait approach motivation with attentional focus after stimulus exposure. Study 4 independently manipulated affect valence and approach motivation strength. Attentional breadth was measured using tasks assessing global versus local processing or eye-tracking patterns.
